blurb: Love-lies-bleeding drapes tall stems in long, drooping ropes of crimson tassels — a dramatic ancient grain crop that doubles as an edible leafy green.
water: Moderate; drought-tolerant once established, deeper water in heat
spread: Single upright annual; self-sows from dropped seed but pulls easily
seasons: Drooping crimson flower tassels mid-summer to first frost; bushy upright foliage spring–summer; killed outright by the first hard freeze
wildlife: Seeds feed finches and other birds; the dense seedheads provide late-season forage
deer: Generally not a deer favorite, but not reliably deer-proof
toxic: No part toxic to people; leaves and seeds are edible cooked. Like all amaranths, foliage can concentrate nitrates when grown on heavily fertilized soil
habitat: Cultivated fields and disturbed ground; the only amaranth grown above ~2,500 m in the Andes

care.hardiness: Grown as a warm-season annual across USDA zones 2–11 — it completes its life in a single season and is killed by the first hard frost, so winter hardiness is not a concern on the Front Range.[1]
care.planting: A frost-tender warm-season annual: do not set plants or sow seed outside until after Boulder's average last frost, roughly May 10–15, and the soil has warmed.[3] You can start seed indoors 4–6 weeks before that and harden off, but amaranth grows so fast from direct seed that sowing in place is simplest. In containers use a deep, well-drained pot in full sun; potted plants dry out far faster in Front Range heat and wind, so check moisture daily. There is no overwintering — the plant dies at the first hard freeze.[1][3]
care.sun: Full sun for the sturdiest, most free-flowering plants; it will grow in partial shade (2–6 hours) but stretches and leans.[1]
care.soil: Average, well-drained soil; tolerates lean and dry ground. Good drainage matters more than fertility — overly rich soil produces lush leaves at the expense of strong flower tassels and can concentrate nitrates in the foliage.[1][2]
care.water: Moderate and drought-tolerant once established. Keep the seedbed evenly moist until seedlings are a few inches tall, then water deeply but infrequently; in the hottest part of a Boulder summer a deep soak every week or so keeps it from wilting. It is listed as drought-resistant and tolerates dry spells.[1]
care.spacing: Thin or space plants 12–18 in. apart (up to ~3 ft for the largest plants); each reaches 1–2 ft wide and 3–5 ft tall.[1]
care.sow: Direct-sow in spring after the last frost (mid-May in Boulder) onto firm, weed-free soil, or start indoors a few weeks earlier. Press the tiny seed into the surface and barely cover. Expect flowering about three months after sowing, so sow promptly for a full summer display.[1][3]
care.depth: Sow shallow — the seed is fine and needs light to germinate, so cover with no more than about 1/4 in. of soil and firm gently.[1]
care.bloom: Mid-summer into fall, with the trailing crimson tassels persisting until the first frost.[1]
care.feeding: Little to none. Skip rich feeding — excess nitrogen makes plants floppy, delays flowering, and increases nitrate accumulation in the leaves; lean soil gives the best plant.[2]
care.maintenance: Low. Tall plants in windy, exposed Front Range sites may need staking. Leave a few seedheads if you want to harvest grain or feed birds; otherwise cut and compost spent plants after frost.[1]
care.selfsow: Self-sows from dropped seed and can return as volunteers, but the seedlings are easy to recognize and pull. This ornamental/grain species is not weedy in the way the wild pigweeds are; thin unwanted spring volunteers.[1]
care.harvest: For grain, cut whole seedheads when they feel dry and rub or shake out the small pale seeds over a sheet; winnow the chaff and store cool and dry. Young leaves can be picked through the growing season for cooked greens.[1][2]
care.propagation: Seed only — this annual cannot be divided or rooted from cuttings. Collect ripe seed from dried heads in late summer/fall and sow the following spring after frost.[1]

edible.summary: Both the grain (seed) and the young leaves are edible — Amaranthus caudatus (kiwicha) is an ancient Andean pseudocereal — but the leaves should be cooked, not eaten raw in quantity.[2][4]
edible.parts: The small seeds are a gluten-free pseudocereal high in protein and lysine; they are ground into flour, popped like tiny popcorn, or cooked as a cereal, and in Mexico popped seeds are bound with honey into the sweet alegría.[4] The young, tender leaves are eaten as a cooked leafy green, mild and spinach-like.[2]
edible.preparation: Cook the leaves — boil or sauté them like spinach (they soften quickly and shouldn't be overcooked). Toast or pop the seeds before eating, or grind them to flour; the raw seed is not eaten as-is.[2][4]
edible.caution: Like all amaranths, the leaves can accumulate oxalates and, when the plant is grown on heavily fertilized (nitrogen-rich) soil, nitrates — so eat leaves cooked and in moderation and avoid leaves from plants pushed with synthetic fertilizer; those with kidney/oxalate concerns should limit them.[2] Be sure of your plant: this is the cultivated grain/ornamental amaranth, not a wild pigweed (do not confuse it with the noxious Palmer amaranth, Amaranthus palmeri, a Colorado-listed weed). No part of A. caudatus is reported poisonous to people.[2][4]
